abstract: This thematic issue explores the diversification and institutionalization of gender equality reforms within parliaments globally. By synthesizing perspectives from both the Global North and South, the collection move beyond traditional debates on descriptive representation to examine the complexities of building gender-sensitive institutions. Utilizing the framework of feminist institutionalism, the contributing authors investigate how internal processes, workplace cultures, and policy outputs are being reshaped to prioritize gender equality. The articles highlight the pivotal role of critical actors—including "feminist academic critical actors"—and supportive coalitions in driving change, while also addressing how local political and cultural contexts can both facilitate and hinder progress. Specific case studies illuminate various challenges, ranging from the impact of autocratic "genderwashing" and the rise of the radical right to the practical difficulties of balancing parliamentary duties with family life. By introducing methodological and conceptual innovations, such as the "most significant change" approach and the study of parliamentary groups as gendered workplaces, this issue offers a comprehensive analysis of the opportunities and barriers to transforming masculinized political spaces into inclusive environments.
